coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots as carriers for intracellular doxorubicin delivery and track
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Qianqian
Ma, Yuan
Che, Mingxuan
Zhang, Boye
Zhang, Yixia
Li, Yi
Zhang, Wendong
Sang, Shengbo
coredata.dc:description: This paper reported a simple pH-responsive fluorescent therapeutic drug delivery system of doxorubicin (DOX)-loaded carbon dots (CDs) for intracellular drug delivery and track in human gastric cancer cells. Drug loading efficiency of this system was 75.3 wt%. The drug release process of this system followed the first-degree drug delivery model, which was ideal for intravenous injection therapy of cancer. The carboxyl-rich CDs had almost no toxicity to human gastric cancer (MGC-803) and human gastric epithelial (GES-1) cells with the survival rates of over 90%. The CDs-DOX system had about a 2-fold inhibitory effect on MGC-803 cells growth compared to GES-1 cells according to the MTT assay. The cell imaging results showed that the CDs-DOX system could be internalized into the cells efficiently. The intracellular uptake and drug delivery efficiency of this system in GES-1 cells was about 30% of that in the MGC-803 cells. The fluorescent CDs enabled the optical labeling and tracking of the drug delivery process at least 48 h. Because of its specific drug delivery and fluorescence tracking properties, the multifunctional CDs-DOX system possesses potential applications in bioimaging, biolabeling and cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
pH-responsive drug delivery system
Tracking drug release
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity assay of CDs, DOX and CDs-DOX complex
Studies on cellular uptake of CDs-DOX complex
The comparision of cellular uptake of CDs-DOX system
The drug tracking capability of fluorescent CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Basic Research Program of Shanxi for Youths

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51505324 , 81602506 , 61471255 , 61474079 ), and Basic Research Program of Shanxi for Youths ( 201701D221111 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, HRTEM (the lower-right inset) and the particle size distribution (the upper-left inset) images of CDs. (b) AFM image of CDs. The upper-left corner inset shows the height profile of the marked line in the AFM image. (c) FTIR spectrum of CDs. (d) Fluorescence spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths. The upper-right corner inset shows photographs of CDs aqueous solution under visible (left) and 365 nm UV light (right).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, DOX and CDs-DOX complex. The inset shows fluorescence spectra of CDs (excitation at 410 nm), DOX (excitation at 480 nm) and CDs-DOX complex (excitation at 410 nm). (b) The surface zeta potential values in deionized water at pH 7.4.
The release process of DOX from the CDs-DOX system versus time at pH 5.0 and 7.4.

Cytotoxicity of CDs with different concentrations for MGC-803 cells (a) and GES-1 cells (b) in 24 h and 48 h. The influence of different concentrations of DOX on the viability of MGC-803 (c1, c2) and GES-1(d1, d2) cells after incubation with DOX and CDs-DOX complex for 24 h and 48 h.

Fluorescence microscopy images of MGC-803 (a) and GES-1 cells (b) incubated with CDs-DOX (5 μg/mL) for 24 h under excitation at 488 nm. (c) Relative pixel intensity of the corresponding fluorescence images in channels of green (CDs) and red (DOX) (the pixel intensity of the MGC-803 cells incubated with the CDs-DOX was defined as 1.0). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Relative pixel intensity of the corresponding fluorescence imaging in channels of green (CDs) and red (DOX) (the pixel intensity of the MGC-803 cells incubated with the CDs-DOX complex (5 μg/mL) for 48 h was defined as 1.0). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the preparation (a) and cellular uptake (b) of the CDs-DOX drug delivery system.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots as carriers for intracellular doxorubicin delivery and track

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MicroNano System Research Center, Key Lab of Advanced Transducers and Intelligent Control System of the Ministry of Education & College of Information and Computer, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Advanced Transducers and Intelligent Control System of the Ministry of Education & College of Information and Computer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Applied Mechanics and Biomedical Engineering & National Demonstration Center for Experimental Mechanics Education, College of Mechanics, Taiyuan University of Technology, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Applied Mechanics and Biomedical Engineering & National Demonstration Center for Experimental Mechanics Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reported a simple pH-responsive fluorescent therapeutic drug delivery system of doxorubicin (DOX)-loaded carbon dots (CDs) for intracellular drug delivery and track in human gastric cancer cells. Drug loading efficiency of this system was 75.3 wt%. The drug release process of this system followed the first-degree drug delivery model, which was ideal for intravenous injection therapy of cancer. The carboxyl-rich CDs had almost no toxicity to human gastric cancer (MGC-803) and human gastric epithelial (GES-1) cells with the survival rates of over 90%. The CDs-DOX system had about a 2-fold inhibitory effect on MGC-803 cells growth compared to GES-1 cells according to the MTT assay. The cell imaging results showed that the CDs-DOX system could be internalized into the cells efficiently. The intracellular uptake and drug delivery efficiency of this system in GES-1 cells was about 30% of that in the MGC-803 cells. The fluorescent CDs enabled the optical labeling and tracking of the drug delivery process at least 48 h. Because of its specific drug delivery and fluorescence tracking properties, the multifunctional CDs-DOX system possesses potential applications in bioimaging, biolabeling and cancer therapy.

A pH-responsive fluorescent therapeutic drug delivery system of doxorubicin (DOX)-loaded carbon dots (CDs) was prepared. The system exhibited superior cancer inhibitory effect and real-time drug tracking capability.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a very critical disease threatening human health and its incidence increases year by year [1]. DOX is an anthracycline antitumor drug commonly used in clinic. It is a cyclical nonspecific drug, which can inhibit cells proliferation and induce cells apoptosis of any growth cycle cells. It disappears rapidly in plasma, and is widely distributed in heart, liver, kidney and other tissues [2]. At present, the inhibitory action of DOX injection on cancer cells is lack of specificity. It can also cause damage to normal cells while killing cancer cells, thus reducing the efficacy of drugs and producing serious side effects, especially heart toxicity [3]. Therefore, the construction of a targeted drug delivery system is critical for cancer treatment.

The drug delivery systems based on nanomaterials have attracted wide attentions and researches in recent years, because they can deliver drugs to the target sites, increase utilization efficiency of drugs, improve curative effect and reduce side effects [4]. In the past few years, various nanoparticles (NPs) including polymeric NPs, iron oxide NPs, Au NPs, mesoporous silica NPs, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and carbon NPs have been utilized as carriers to deliver anticancer drugs for cancer therapy [5–10]. Unfortunately, these nanoparticles present several disadvantages, such as large particle size, high toxicity, low biocompatibility, poor water solubility and awful fluorescence properties. These defects restrict their applications in cancer treatment.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new kind of fluorescent nanomaterial, own its unique advantages, such as small particle size, good water solubility, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, well cell permeability, tunable emission wavelength, strong fluorescence emission, high photostability, anti-photobleaching, rich surface functional groups and ease of surface functionalization [11,12]. Furthermore, various precursor materials, preparation methods and heteroatom doping schemes have been developed for facile synthesis of CDs, and high fluorescence quantum yield values have been obtained [13–16] (See Table S1). Based on these advantages, carbon dots are widely applied in energy conversion, photocatalysis, fluorescent probes, drug delivery and bioimaging [16–19]. Anticancer agents (mitomycin, boldine, doxorubicin, flutamide, oxaliplatin and cisplatin) have combined with different carbon dots to form various fluorescent drug delivery systems [15,16,20–24]. These drug delivery systems can not only retain their original anticancer function, but also be tracked by the fluorescence originating from carbon dots, suggesting the application prospect of fluorescent carbon dots in cancer therapy.

In this work, water-soluble carbon dots rich in carboxyl groups were synthesized from citric acid and urea by simple microwave irradiation. The as-prepared CDs had an average size of 2.6 nm and emitted green fluorescence with a fluorescence quantum yield of 16.7%. The CDs had good biocompatibility and could be easily internalized by cells. CDs and DOX combined through electrostatic interaction with drug loading efficiency of 75.3 wt% (Scheme 1 a). The drug release in vitro, cytotoxicity assay and cell imaging were studied to evaluate the efficacy of the pH-responsive CDs-DOX drug delivery system. The DOX could be released from CDs-DOX system depending on the weakly acidic pH value in cancer cells and easily internalized into the nucleus (Scheme 1b). The CDs-DOX system had effective inhibitory effect on MGC-803 cells and low side effects to GES-1 cells. And the CDs-DOX system showed the traceability of drug delivery in real time.

Citric acid, urea, rhodamine 6G and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Sinopharm Ltd, China. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX∙HCl), 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and dialysis bag MD34 (molecular weight cut-off 3500 Da) were purchased from Shanghai Chuang Sai Science and Technology Ltd, China. The Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 culture medium was purchased from Mike Ward Biology Co., Ltd, China. All chemicals were used in the absence of further purification. Water used in all experiments was obtained by a Milli-Q water system.

A high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEM 2010, 200kv, Japan) and atomic force microscope (AFM) (Park Systems NX10, Korea) were used to characterize the morphology of the CDs. The surface functional groups and chemical compositions of samples were tested by fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (BRUKER OPTIK GmbH Tensor27, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi America). The absorption spectra were measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (METASH UV-8000A, 190 nm-1200 nm, China). The fluorescence spectra, fluorescence lifetime and photoluminescence quantum yield were measured by FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (GDINBURGH INSTRUMENTS, Britain). The zeta potentials were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern ZS90, Britain). The optical density (OD) values for toxicity testing were obtained by Microplate Reader (Thermo Scientific MULTISKAN FC, China). In vitro cell imaging was studied by fluorescence microscope (Leica Microsystems LEICA DM 3000, China).

Citric acid (1 g) and urea (2 g) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, and stirred slowly until transparency. Then the solution was heated in a domestic 720 W microwave oven for 5 min. In the process, the color of solution changed from colorless to brown, and finally formed a dark-brown clustered solid, indicating the formation of CDs. The as-prepared CDs were dissolved in a small amount of water, and centrifuged (6000 r/min, 7 min) to remove solid precipitate and obtain supernatant solution. The supernatant solution was then transferred to a vacuum oven and heated at 60 °C for one day. Finally, the pure CDs were obtained.

1 mg/mL of CDs and 1 mg/mL of DOX were mixed with the same volume, and then the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.4. Subsequently, the mixed solution was under magnetic stirring at 37 °C for overnight. In order to remove unloaded DOX, the mixture was dialyzed against deionized water in the dark for 24 h and then the component CDs-DOX solution was stored in 4 °C. The amount of unbound DOX was calculated by UV absorbance at 484 nm according to the calibration curve of DOX in deionized water (Fig. S1). Then the drug loading efficiency could be obtained.

10 mL of CDs-DOX solution was packed into a dialysis bag and put into 100 mL of PBS solution with different pH values (5.0 and 7.4). Then, the releasing process was performed in a water bath with magnetic stirring at 37 °C. At timed intervals, the released amount of DOX was calculated by UV absorbance at 484 nm according to the calibration curve of DOX in the PBS solutions of pH 5.0 and 7.4, respectively (Fig. S1). Drug cumulative released ratio was expressed as the percentage of drug amount released by dialysis membrane over time to the total drug amount.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity assay of CDs, DOX and CDs-DOX complex
ce:section.ce:para: MGC-803 and GES-1 cells were cultured at 37 °C in a humidified environment with 5% CO2. The RPMI 1640 culture medium for cells culture contained 5% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin-streptomycin solution. Cell viability was determined using the MTT assay. The cells were seeded at a density of 2 × 104 cells per well in 96-wells culture plates and cultured until 80% of cells adhered to the wall. The cells were treated with CDs, DOX and CDs-DOX complex at different concentrations and followed by an additional 24 h or 48 h culture. Untreated cells were used as a control group. After 24 h and 48 h culture, MTT solution (10 μL, 5 mg/mL) was added to each of wells, and put in the incubator for 4 h. Finally, 100 μL of DMSO was added to dissolve the bluish violet crystals, and the absorbance value of each well was measured at 450 nm by Microplate Reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on cellular uptake of CDs-DOX complex
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the cellular uptake and intracellular drug release of CDs-DOX drug delivery system in normal and cancer cells, the fluorescence imaging of MGC-803 and GES-1 cells treated with CDs-DOX complex was observed. The cells were seeded on sterile coverslips in 24-wells culture plates. After cells adherence, the old medium was discarded. Then, fresh medium with CDs-DOX complex was added in the cells. After incubation for 24 h, the supernatant was discarded and the cells were carefully washed three times with PBS. Subsequently, the cells were fixed with 1 mL of 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min and cleaned twice with PBS again. The coverslips were taken out and the cells were observed by fluorescence microscope under the same conditions. In order to study the intracellular uptake and drug release process of CDs-DOX drug delivery system, the fluorescence imaging of MGC-803 cells treated with CDs-DOX complex at different time was observed. The method of fluorescence imaging was the same as above.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, CDs had good dispersivity and approximate spherical structure with an average size of 2.6 nm. The HRTEM image showed the lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.31 nm, which was consistent with the spacing between graphene layers (002) facet [25]. The height profile of AFM image in Fig. 1b showed that the height of CDs ranged from 1.98 nm to 3.45 nm and the average height was about 2.6 nm. As depicted in Fig. 1c, broad absorption peaks at 3050-3552 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H bonds [23,26]. Absorption peaks at 2808 cm−1 and 1411 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration and bending vibration of C-H bond in the CDs, respectively [27,28]. Absorption peaks from 1640 cm−1 to 1714 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C=O bond of carboxyl groups [26]. The absorption peak at 1614 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C=N bond [14,29]. Furthermore, the negligible peak at 1191 cm−1 belonged to the asymmetrical stretching vibration of the C–O–C bond [30]. The sharp peak at 1064 cm−1 was possibly ascribed to the C-O bond of carboxyl groups [30]. FTIR spectrum confirmed that the CDs were nitrogen doped and carboxyl-rich groups on the surface, which was in line with the previous reports [11,26]. The results from FTIR analysis were further demonstrated by the XPS spectra (Fig. S2). Therefore, the as-prepared CDs had good water solubility and could smoothly interact with the amino moiety of DOX via electrostatic adsorption [11,28,31]. As described in Fig. 1d, the CDs emitted strong green fluorescence under UV light. When the excitation wavelength shifted from 350 nm to 550 nm, the emission peak had a slightly red shift, owing to the varied band gaps from different surface states of CDs [32]. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was strongest at 450 nm excitation. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was measured to be 16.7% (Fig. S3 and Table S2). The fluorescence intensity and peak position almost remained stable over a wide pH range from 3 to 10 (Fig. S4), indicating superior photostability for biomedical applications [13,29,33].

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs, DOX and CDs-DOX complex are shown in Fig. 2 a. The UV–vis absorption of CDs displayed two clear peaks at 328 nm and 410 nm, which was attributed to π-π bonds conjugated structure [11]. The UV–vis absorption of DOX presented a clear peak at 480 nm. After the combination of CDs and DOX, the UV–vis absorption of CDs-DOX complex included obvious peaks at 328 nm and 410 nm, and also a small peak at 480 nm. The CDs showed a strong fluorescence peak at 523 nm under excitation at 410 nm. The fluorescence spectra of DOX revealed a characteristic peak at 590 nm under excitation at 480 nm. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-DOX complex decreased compared with the CDs. It could be deduced preliminarily that the DOX had combined with the CDs according to the absorption and fluorescence spectra. It should be noted that the overlap between CDs emission and DOX absorption maybe generate the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process [11,25,34,35]. However, the fluorescence lifetime values of CDs in the presence and absence of DOX remained almost the same (Fig. S5), which excluded the possibility of FRET [11,25,35]. The decrease of the CDs emission may result from the inner filter effect and the decrease of the absorption of CDs [11,25,35].

The zeta potential was used to further prove the binding of CDs and DOX. As shown in Fig. 2b, the surface potential of CDs and DOX were −37.3 mV and 18.57 mV, respectively. After the combination of CDs and DOX, the surface potential of CDs-DOX complex was 7.29 mV, which was attributed to the occurrence of charge neutralization between CDs and DOX [11]. In PBS solution of pH 7.4, the amino of DOX protonated with positive charges and the carboxyl of CDs deprotonated with a large number of negative charges, indicating a strong electrostatic interaction between DOX and CDs [11,22,36,37]. Moreover, the π-π stacking interaction may also contribute to the binding [22,37]. The drug loading efficiency of CDs-DOX system reached 75.3 wt% (Fig. S6), which was comparable to other reported drug delivery systems (Table S1) [20,21,23,38,39].

Fig. 3 shows the DOX cumulative released ratio versus time at pH 5.0 and 7.4. Obviously, the release amount of DOX was considerably lower at pH 7.4 compared with pH 5.0. It exhibited a sustained and stable release about 8 h at pH 5.0 and then reached a plateau. The drug release amount of pH 5.0 was about fourfold higher than that of pH 7.4. With the decrease of pH value, the carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs were gradually protonated, and the electrostatic interaction between DOX and CDs weakened gradually. Then the release rate and release amount of DOX increased obviously. The release trend of DOX was simulated by four of the most common drug release models, and it was found that both of the curves followed first-degree drug release model [40]. The drug release process maintained a steady release rate and the drug amount in the body could remain constant according to the first-degree model [41]. On the other hand, the first-degree model meant the CDs-DOX drug delivery system had a well solubility [40]. At pH 5.0 and 7.4, the rate constant of first-degree release were 0.53 and 0.84, respectively. This indicated that the release rate of DOX at pH 5.0 was larger than that of 7.4, and the tumor weakly acidic environment was beneficial for rapider release of DOX [41]. The maximum cumulative released ratios of DOX were 79.0% and 19.9% corresponding to pH 5.0 and 7.4. The result demonstrates that the CDs-DOX system is an effective pH-sensitive drug delivery system.

In order to verify the effectiveness and safety of the CDs-DOX system before it was put into practical application, the MGC-803 cells and GES-1 cells were used for toxicity test in vitro. These two cell lines were incubated with a series of different concentrations of CDs, DOX and CDs-DOX complex for 24 and 48 h. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the CDs had almost no influence on the viability of cell growth within the investigated concentration range. Cell viability was over 90% compared to the control groups even when the incubation time reached 48 h and the concentration of CDs was 100 μg/mL. Therefore, the as-prepared carboxyl-rich CDs are benignant to these two cell lines and can be used as a fluorescent probe for cell imaging and tracking drug release.

Then the cytotoxicity of free DOX and CDs-DOX complex in cancerous and normal gastric cells was compared. As shown in Fig. 4c, both DOX and CDs-DOX complex caused dosage-dependent death of MGC-803 cells. About 50% MGC-803 cells incubated with DOX and CDs-DOX complex were killed after 24 h at a DOX concentration of 5 μg/mL. The cell viability was lower when the co-culture time extended to 48 h. Free DOX and CDs-DOX complex inhibited the growth of MGC-803 cells with similar trend in vitro. However, the cytotoxicity results of GES-1 cells cultured with CDs-DOX complex were significantly different from that of MGC-803 cells. As shown in Fig. 4d, when the concentration of DOX was 5 μg/mL, about 50% GES-1 cells incubated with DOX for 24 h were killed, but the survival rate of GES-1 cells incubated with CDs-DOX complex was more than 85%. The cytotoxicity of CDs-DOX complex to GES-1 cells was almost half of that of free DOX. The result indicated that the CDs-DOX system could inhibit the cancer cells growth efficiently and reduce adverse side effects to the normal cells. That was because the DOX can be more smoothly released from the CDs-DOX system in cancer cells due to the mildly acidic environment [11]. The results of cytotoxicity test were consistent with those of drug release shown in Fig. 3.

To obtain the quantization parameter of the clinical application, the half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) values of DOX and CDs-DOX drug delivery system were computed. The IC50 values for DOX and CDs-DOX system in MGC-803 cells at 24 h were 4.61 μg/mL and 4.93 μg/mL, respectively (Fig. S7). The corresponding IC50 values at 48 h were 2.94 μg/mL and 3.17 μg/mL. The results indicated that the IC50 value of CDs-DOX system was just a little higher than that of DOX, and the inhibitory effect of CDs-DOX system was comparable with free DOX at the same concentration for gastric cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The comparision of cellular uptake of CDs-DOX system
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve more qualitative description of CDs-DOX complex internalization and contrast between cancer and normal cells, the fluorescence microscopy imaging of MGC-803 and GES-1 cells was carried out. As shown in Fig. 5 a. The MGC-803 cells clearly showed fluorescence signals in the green and red channels under excitation at 488 nm. The cells cultured with CDs just presented green fluorescence signal under excitation at 488 nm (Fig. S8). It could be deduced that the red fluorescence signal was from DOX. The fluorescence imaging results indicated that CDs-DOX system was internalized into MGC-803 cells efficiently, and the DOX could smoothly be released from CDs-DOX system and endocytosed into the cell nucleus, which was in agreement with other reports [42]. As shown in Fig. 5b, GES-1 cells showed weak fluorescence signals in both channels compared to MGC-803 cells, which indicated that it was difficult for CDs-DOX system to internalize into GES-1 cells, and DOX could hardly be released from CDs-DOX system.

In order to quantitatively and intuitively compare the uptake and release of CDs-DOX system in MGC-803 and GES-1 cells, the cell imaging was analyzed by ImageJ software [42]. The relative fluorescent pixel intensities in MGC-803 cells were about 3.2 and 3.5 times of that in GES-1 cells for green and red channels, respectively (Fig. 5c). The results indicated the more effective internalization of CDs-DOX and the favourable release of DOX in MGC-803 cells. The different imaging results between normal and cancer cells are attributed to the different cell characteristics and intracellular pH environments [11]. The results in Fig. 5 were consistent with the results shown in Fig. 3. Compared to other complicated drug delivery systems, the cellular uptake and intracellular drug release of the simple CDs-DOX drug delivery system also worked fairly well [20,37,38]. Therefore, the CDs-DOX system displays great capacity of drug delivery in cancer cells, and can be used as a fluorescence nanoprobe for tracking drug release simultaneously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The drug tracking capability of fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To study the cellular uptake and intracellular drug release process of CDs-DOX system over time, and evaluate the effective fluorescent tracking time, the fluorescence microscopy imaging of MGC-803 cells at different time was observed and analyzed quantitatively by ImageJ software. As shown in Fig. 6 , the overall trends of relative pixel intensities in green (CDs) and red (DOX) channels were growing, excepting for 36 h. This indicated that the uptake and drug release amount of CDs-DOX system increased gradually. And the CDs-DOX system might be excreted and metabolized by MGC-803 cells at 36 h. According the results of the cell imaging, the CDs could achieve fluorescence tracking for drug delivery at least 48 h (Fig. S9). In addition, the cell morphology became round and shrunken after 24 h, and the cells were gradually approaching apoptosis (Fig. S9). This intuitively showed the inhibitory effect of CDs-DOX drug delivery system on MGC-803 cells and the real-time drug tracking capability of fluorescent CDs.

A pH-responsive drug delivery system was established based on the low toxic fluorescent CDs as carriers for loading of DOX. The CDs-DOX system had high drug loading efficiency of 75.3 wt%. The drug release amount of pH 5.0 was about fourfold higher than that of pH 7.4. This system showed sustained and steady drug release in weakly acidic environment of pH 5.0. The cell viability treated with CDs-DOX system was comparable with that of free DOX in MGC-803 cells. However, the former was twice over the latter in GES-1 cells. The cellular uptake and intracellular drug delivery efficiency of CDs-DOX system in GES-1 cells was about 30% of that in MGC-803 cells. The uptake and drug release process of CDs-DOX system was observed over 48 h. DOX could be released gradually into the cell nucleus and tracked in real-time by the fluorescence of CDs. The effective inhibitory action of the drug delivery system to the cancer cells and the reduction of the side-effect to the normal cells were demonstrated. The fluorescent CDs-DOX drug delivery system is promising in the fields of cancer therapy and drug real-time tracking.
There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51505324, 81602506, 61471255, 61474079), and Basic Research Program of Shanxi for Youths (201701D221111).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jddst.2018.12.015.
